serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ume Juice is approximately 350 ml (350 g), equivalent to a medium-sized takeaway cup as served at KOI Thé and similar Taiwanese beverage chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Plum juice, canned — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 168185)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for ume juice (Prunus mume juice) as served by Taiwanese beverage chains. USDA SR Legacy 'Plum juice, canned' (FDC ID 168185) is used as the closest approved analogue. Ume (green plum) juice is compositionally similar to plum juice: fruit-acid-dominant, moderate sugar, negligible protein and fat. Values are adapted to reflect a diluted commercial beverage preparation typical of Taiwanese chain formats (approximately 50–60% juice concentration with added water and syrup), resulting in slightly lower energy and sugar than undiluted canned plum juice. All adaptations are conservative and within the plausible range for this product category.

gi_source.title: Plum, raw — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney (GI = 40)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ume juice (Prunus mume juice) is available in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of raw plum (GI ≈ 40, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database is used as the closest defensible analogue. Ume (green plum) is botanically related to plum (Prunus domestica) and shares a similar organic-acid and sugar profile. The juice form may have a marginally higher GI than whole fruit due to removal of fibre, but remains in the low GI range. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 32.2) / 100 ≈ 13.0, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 9.2 × 3.5 = 32.2 g.

hypertension.guidance: Ume juice contains approximately 5 mg sodium per 100 ml and provides modest potassium (~95 mg/100 ml), making it a low-sodium beverage that does not adversely affect blood pressure when consumed in standard portions; however, individuals should be mindful of added sugar content in commercial preparations.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Free sugar content and glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although ume juice has a low glycemic index (~40), a standard 350 ml serving delivers approximately 32 g of carbohydrate (predominantly free sugars), yielding a glycemic load of ~13; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, choose unsweetened or reduced-sugar formulations, and account for this beverage within their total daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat; moderate free sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ume juice is essentially fat-free and contains no saturated fat, posing minimal direct cardiovascular lipid risk; however, regular consumption of sweetened commercial versions contributes free sugars that may adversely affect triglyceride levels and overall cardiometabolic risk if consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ume Juice (青梅汁, Qīng méi zhī) is a fruit-based beverage made from ume, the green or semi-ripe fruit of Prunus mume, a species closely related to both the Japanese plum and apricot. The drink is popular across East and Southeast Asia, particularly in Taiwan, Japan, and China, and has gained a following in Singapore through Taiwanese beverage chains such as KOI Thé. The juice is characteristically tart and refreshing, with a distinctive sour-sweet flavour profile derived from the high organic acid content (citric and malic acids) of the ume fruit, balanced by added sugar or syrup in commercial preparations. The colour ranges from pale yellow-green to light amber depending on the ripeness of the fruit and the degree of dilution. In commercial chain formats, ume juice is typically served chilled or over ice, often with adjustable sweetness levels. Nutritionally, ume juice is a low-to-moderate energy beverage (~38 kcal/100 ml) with negligible protein and fat, and a carbohydrate content of approximately 9 g/100 ml, predominantly from free sugars. It provides modest potassium and small amounts of vitamin C and iron. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), though the glycemic load per standard 350 ml serving is moderate (~13) due to the volume of free sugars consumed. Clinically, ume juice is suitable for most healthy adults in moderate portions but warrants caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome due to its free sugar content. It is naturally free of gluten, dairy, and common allergens, and is suitable for vegetarian and vegan diets.
common_user_queries: How many calories in ume juice?
Is ume juice good for diabetics?
What does ume juice taste like?
Is KOI ume juice high in sugar?
Is ume juice the same as plum juice?
Can I drink ume juice if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: accompaniment to Taiwanese or East Asian meals

confidence_reason: No direct approved-source entry for ume juice (Prunus mume juice) as a commercial Taiwanese beverage chain product exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy 'Plum juice, canned' (FDC ID 168185), which is the closest approved analogue. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for raw plum (GI ≈ 40). Both adaptations are scientifically defensible but introduce moderate uncertainty, justifying a medium confidence rating.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy 'Plum juice, canned' (FDC ID 168185) and adjusted downward to reflect the diluted, commercially sweetened format typical of Taiwanese beverage chains (~50–60% juice concentration). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy plum juice entry. 3. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy plum juice entry. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source for this beverage form. 5. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 6. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 7. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source; ume juice is a plant-based beverage and vitamin B12 is naturally zero by nature. 10. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 11. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source; vitamin D is naturally zero in fruit juice beverages. 12. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 13. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 14. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 15. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 16. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 17. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plum juice contains no saturated fat per USDA SR Legacy). 18. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no trans fats in fruit juice). 19. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based beverage, naturally zero cholesterol). 20. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for raw plum as the closest approved analogue; glycemic_index set to 40 as an analogous estimate. 21. The food_name 'M-Ume Juice' from the input is a menu-style label used by KOI Thé; the canonical English name 'Ume Juice' is used in food_name. 'M-Ume Juice' is retained as an alias. 22. This is a Taiwanese-cuisine beverage, not a Singaporean dish; therefore, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi mandatory multilingual fields do not apply under the cuisine-based multilingual rules. Chinese and Japanese names are provided as the primary relevant local-language forms. 23. Serving size of 350 g is estimated based on standard medium-cup format at Taiwanese beverage chains in Singapore.
